content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : LEGAL DIVISION, JOHN K., JAY, ALAN,
NAN, JOHN G.

Date : July 11, 1977

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
Gina

Subject: Filing Requirements


There has been quite a lot of discussion recently about
"secondary" filing requirements. The following are three
specific examples about which the Secretary of State has
asked our advice.

1. A corporation or company has wholly owned subsidiaries.
The subsidiaries contribute $5,000 or more to the parent
company. The parent company in turn makes all contri-
butions to candidates and committees.

QUESTION: Must the wholly owned subsidiaries file
as major donors when they contribute
$5,000 to the parent company for a
political purpose? No the parent end its subs should
file one consolidated major donor statement.

2. A state association receives funds from county members of
the association, the funds represent a portion of the dues
received by the county association from members. The
portion of the dues sent to the state has been earmarked
by the members to be used for political contributions
however no candidates have been specified. The county
makes no direct contribution. The state association is a
registered political action committee.

QUESTION: Is the county association a recipient committee or
an intermediary? See [illegible]

(2)

Legal Division, John K., Jay, Alan,
Nan, John G.                                    -2-                    July 11, 1977


3.    This third question I believe we resolved at the opinion
      request meeting of July 1.

      A state organization (The Malt Beverage Benevolent Association)
      receives contributions from member companies (Coors, etc.) who
      have reason to know that a portion of those funds will be used
      for political purposes.

      QUESTION: When that member company contributes $5,000 or
      more to the state association must it also file as
      a major donor? [?]

      Opinion request meeting participants decided yes.it should.
      It seems clear that in the first example there would be no
      filing obligation by a subsidiary company but the second
      and third example is not so clear cut, certainly in the
      third example we have in fact decided that the member companies
      do have a filing obligation.

      The Secretary of State has recently sent letters to companies
      and associations in all of the above categories who did not
      file as major donors after contributing $5,000 or more to a
      parent company or state organization which ultimately made
      political contributions.

      How is the Secretary of State to make a determination as to
      which category any of the "would be filers" are in? The
      Secretary of State would like to have our advice on how they
      should proceed in requiring certain of the above companies or
      local associations to file. Also, should they be required to
      file in connection with each election contributed to or on
      an off-year basis?

sections.conclusion: The document indicates that wholly owned subsidiaries, county associations, and member organizations do have filing obligations. The Secretary of State would like guidance on how to proceed in requiring these local associations to file and clarifications on filing procedures in connection with each type of entity on a year-to-year basis.
sections.question_synthetic: The FPPC is providing advice to the California Secretary of State regarding three scenarios involving secondary filing requirements under political finance laws, specifically whether subsidiary corporations, county associations, and member organizations must file as major donors when funds flow through parent entities or state organizations to candidates and committees.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC's preliminary position suggests that secondary entities (subsidiaries, county associations, and member organizations) are subject to filing requirements as major donors when contributions flow through them, though the Secretary of State seeks clarification on specific filing procedures and timelines.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document quality is poor with significant OCR errors and missing text throughout. The structure suggests this is an FPPC advice letter with questions and preliminary opinion, but the conclusion section is incomplete and heavily degraded. Confidence is moderate due to text quality issues, though the core document type and general content are identifiable.

CONCLUSION: The document indicates that wholly owned subsidiaries, county associations, and member organizations do have filing obligations. The Secretary of State would like guidance on how to proceed in requiring these local associations to file and clarifications on filing procedures in connection with each type of entity on a year-to-year basis.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Memorandum To : LEGAL DIVISION, JOHN K., JAY, ALAN, NAN, JOHN G. Date : July 11, 1977 From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Gina Subject: Filing Requirements There has been quite a lot of discussion recently about "secondary" filing requirements. The following are three specific examples about which the Secretary of State has asked our advice. 1. A corporation or company has wholly owned subsidiaries. The subsidiaries contribute $5,000 or more to the parent company. The parent company in turn makes all contri- butions to candidates and committees. QUESTION: Must the wholly owned subsidiaries file as major donors when they contribute $5,000 to the parent company for a political purpose? No the parent end its subs should file one consolidated major donor statement. 2. A state association receives funds from county members of the association, the funds represent a portion of the dues received by the county association from members. The portion of the dues sent to the state has been earmarked by the members to be used for political contributions however no candidates have been specified. The county makes no direct contribution. The state association is a registered political action committee. QUESTION: Is the county association a recipient committee or an intermediary? See [illegible] (2) Legal Division, John K., Jay, Alan, Nan, John G. -2- July 11, 1977 3. This third question I believe we resolved at the opinion request meeting of July 1. A state organization (The Malt Beverage Benevolent Association) receives contributions from member companies (Coors, etc.) who have reason to know that a portion of those funds will be used for political purposes. QUESTION: When that member company contributes $5,000 or more to the state association must it also file as a major donor? [?] Opinion request meeting participants decided yes.it should. It seems clear that in the first example there would be no filing obligation by a subsidiary company but the second and third example is not so clear cut, certainly in the third example we have in fact decided that the member companies do have a filing obligation. The Secretary of State has recently sent letters to companies and associations in all of the above categories who did not file as major donors after contributing $5,000 or more to a parent company or state organization which ultimately made political contributions. How is the Secretary of State to make a determination as to which category any of the "would be filers" are in? The Secretary of State would like to have our advice on how they should proceed in requiring certain of the above companies or local associations to file. Also, should they be required to file in connection with each election contributed to or on an off-year basis?
embedding.summary: The FPPC provides advice to the California Secretary of State on three complex scenarios involving secondary filing requirements for political contributions, specifically addressing whether subsidiary corporations, county associations, and member organizations must file as major donors when funds flow through parent or state entities to political candidates and committees.
